1: hallĕlû-yāh hallĕlû ʾet-šēm YHWH hallĕlû ʿabdê YHWH — "praise Yah! Praise the name of the LORD! Praise, O servants of the LORD!" — the triple hallĕlû in one verse is emphatic; the imperative plural presses urgency and communal weight. hallĕlû-yāh (praise Yah) combines hālal (to praise, shine, boast) with the shortened divine name Yāh. šēm YHWH (name of the LORD) — in Hebrew thought the name is not merely a label but the person-in-action; to praise the name is to praise YHWH as he has revealed and enacted himself.
2: šeʿōmĕdîm bĕbêt YHWH bĕḥaṣrôt bêt ʾĕlōhênû — "who stand in the house of the LORD, in the courts of the house of our God" — ʿāmad (stand) again as the technical term for priestly service (cf. Ps 134:1). The verse mirrors 134:1 deliberately: the servants who stand at night are called to praise. ḥaṣrôt (courts) — the outer courts where the broader assembly gathered; the distinction from the inner house marks the range of the temple complex. ʾĕlōhênû (our God) — the possessive pronoun marks the covenantal relationship distinguishing Israel from those whose gods are foreign.
3: hallĕlû-yāh kî-ṭôb YHWH zammĕrû lĕšimô kî nāʿîm — "praise the LORD, for the LORD is good; sing praises to his name, for it is delightful/pleasant" — two grounds for praise: kî-ṭôb (for he is good) — the most fundamental theological assertion, echoing Ps 106:1, 107:1, 118:1, and 136:1; and kî nāʿîm (for it is pleasant/lovely). nāʿîm (pleasant, lovely, from the same root as Naomi's name) — praise of YHWH is presented as aesthetically satisfying, not merely obligatory duty.
4: kî-yaʿăqōb bāḥar lô yāh yiśrāʾēl lisĕgullātô — "for the LORD chose Jacob as his own, Israel as his prized possession" — bāḥar (Qal perfect, chose) — divine election is the first ground of national praise: praise flows from being chosen. sĕgullāh (H5459) — a treasured possession kept close; the word appears in the covenant-making contexts of Exod 19:5, Deut 7:6, 14:2, and 26:18, always for Israel as YHWH's specially held possession among all peoples. lô yāh — "for himself, for Yah" — the election is not for Israel's benefit alone but for YHWH's purposes and ownership.
5: kî ʾănî yādaʿtî kî-gādôl YHWH waʾădōnênû mikkol-ʾĕlōhîm — "for I know that the LORD is great — our Lord stands above every so-called god" — ʾănî yādaʿtî (I personally know) — the shift to first-person singular gives a confessional force; this is not abstract theology but personal knowledge. gādôl YHWH mikkol-ʾĕlōhîm (the LORD is great above all gods) — the superlative comparison does not affirm the existence of other gods but asserts incomparable supremacy (see Exod 18:11 for the same form).
6: kōl ʾăšer ḥāpēṣ YHWH ʿāśāh bašāmayim ûbāʾāreṣ bāyammîm wĕkol-tĕhōmôt — "the LORD does whatever he pleases — in heaven and on earth, in the seas and every depth" — kōl ʾăšer ḥāpēṣ ʿāśāh (everything that he delights in he does) — divine sovereignty expressed as unrestricted will. ḥāpēṣ (delight in, desire, take pleasure in) — YHWH's actions are not mechanical necessity but volitional delight. The four zones — heaven, earth, seas, depths (tĕhōmôt) — constitute the full created order in its ancient three-tier model, asserting total sovereignty without exception.
7: maʿăleh nĕśiʾîm miqqĕṣēh hāʾāreṣ bĕrāqîm lammāṭār ʿāśāh môṣēʾ-rûaḥ mēʾôṣĕrōtāyw — "he draws up clouds from the ends of the earth, makes lightning flash with the rain, and releases the wind from his storehouses" — three participles depict ongoing governance of weather: maʿăleh (causing clouds to ascend), ʿāśāh bĕrāqîm (making lightning), môṣēʾ-rûaḥ (bringing out wind). ʾôṣĕrôt (storehouses, treasuries) — ancient cosmology imagined reserves of rain, wind, and snow held by God (Job 38:22; Jer 10:13). The theological point: weather belongs to YHWH, not Baal the Canaanite storm deity.
8: šehhikkāh bĕkôrê miṣrayim mēʾādām ʿad-bĕhēmāh — "he struck down the firstborn of Egypt — both people and animals alike" — hikkāh (Hiphil perfect of nākāh, to strike, smite) — the same verb used throughout the plague narratives (Exod 7–12). The scope mēʾādām ʿad-bĕhēmāh (from human to beast) deliberately echoes Exod 11:5 and 12:12, tying the historical credo exactly to the Passover narrative. The verse moves from creation sovereignty (vv6–7) to redemptive history: the same power governing the cosmos acted in Egypt.
9: šālaḥ ʾôtôt ûmôpĕtîm bĕtôkēk miṣrayim bĕparʿōh ûbĕkol-ʿăbādāyw — "he sent miraculous signs and wonders into Egypt — upon Pharaoh and all his servants" — ʾôtôt ûmôpĕtîm (signs and wonders) — the paired formula for the plagues (Deut 4:34; 6:22; 7:19; 26:8); so fixed in the tradition that the Exodus is regularly summarized by this phrase alone. ʿăbādāyw (his servants/officials) — the term for Pharaoh's court officials parallels the term for YHWH's servants (v1), creating an ironic contrast: the true servants (Israel) versus Pharaoh's servile court.
10: šehhikkāh gôyim rabbîm wĕhārag mĕlākîm ʿăṣûmîm — "he defeated great nations and put mighty kings to death" — the historical credo moves from Exodus to conquest. rabbîm (great, many) and ʿăṣûmîm (mighty, powerful) — the emphasis on the power of the defeated heightens the contrast with YHWH's supreme power. The verse introduces the list of conquered kings in vv11–12.
11: lĕSîḥôn melek hāʾĕmōrî ûlĕʿôg melek habbāšān wĕlĕkōl mamlĕkôt kĕnāʿan — "Sihon king of the Amorites, Og king of Bashan, and all the kingdoms of Canaan" — Sihon and Og are the archetypal defeated kings of the conquest narrative (Num 21:21–35; Deut 2–3). They appear together in the historical creeds of Pss 135–136, Neh 9:22, and Deut 31:4. Their defeat east of the Jordan was the first conquest milestone; "all the kingdoms of Canaan" extends the record through the period of Joshua.
12: wĕnātan ʾarṣām nāḥălāh nāḥălāh lĕyiśrāʾēl ʿammô — "and gave their land as an inheritance — an inheritance to his people Israel" — the repeated nāḥălāh (inheritance, heritage) — the doubling (first as cognate accusative, then in apposition) is emphatic: the land given is truly and permanently an inheritance. nāḥălāh carries legal and familial weight — not a gift to be returned but a permanent family possession tied to covenant identity (Num 26:52–56; Josh 13–22).
13: YHWH šimĕkā lĕʿôlām YHWH zikrĕkā lĕdōr-wādōr — "O LORD, your name endures forever; your memory endures through every generation" — šēm (name) and zēker (memory, memorial, H2143) are paired as near-synonyms: the name is how YHWH is known; the zēker is how he is remembered and re-presented. This verse is a near-quotation of Exod 3:15, where YHWH declares his name to Moses: "this is my name forever, and this is my memorial unto all generations." Psalm 135 explicitly grounds praise of YHWH's name in the Sinai self-disclosure.
14: kî-yādîn YHWH ʿammô wĕʿal-ʿăbādāyw yitnaḥēm — "the LORD will uphold the cause of his people and will show compassion toward his servants" — yādîn (Qal imperfect of dîn, to judge, vindicate, plead the case of) — the imperfect expresses ongoing and future advocacy; YHWH as juridical defender. yitnaḥēm (Hithpael of nāḥam, to show compassion, relent, be moved to pity) — the Hithpael expresses inner emotional movement; this is not cold judicial process but the compassion of a parent moved for their children (cf. Hos 11:8). The verse closely parallels Deut 32:36.
15: ʿăṣabbê haggôyim kesep wĕzāhāb maʿăśēh yĕdê ʾādām — "the idols of the nations are silver and gold — the work of human hands" — this verse opens the anti-idol polemic (vv15–18) adapted from Ps 115:4–8. ʿăṣabbê (idols, from ʿāṣab — to fashion, carve, also to grieve) — the term carries the sense of painful labor producing a lifeless product. maʿăśēh yĕdê ʾādām (work of human hands) — the decisive theological critique: the idol is human manufacture, not divine self-disclosure. What is made cannot be greater than its maker.
16: peh-lāhem wĕlōʾ yĕdabbērû ʿênayim lāhem wĕlōʾ yirʾû — "they have mouths, but they cannot speak; they have eyes, but they cannot see" — the parallelism proceeds through the sensory organs: mouth (speech), eyes (sight), ears (hearing, v17), breath (v17). In each case the idol has the anatomical form without the function. In the ancient world, the idol's mouth was ritually "opened" in the Babylonian mīs pî ceremony to animate it with divine presence. The psalm denies that any such animation occurs: the opened mouth remains speechless.
17: ʾoznayim lāhem wĕlōʾ yaʾăzînû ʾap ʾên-yēš-rûaḥ bĕpîhem — "they have ears, but they do not hear; there is not a breath of air in their mouths" — rûaḥ (spirit/breath/wind) — the word encompasses the animating breath of life (Gen 2:7; Ezek 37:5). The idol has no rûaḥ: it cannot breathe, cannot be animated, cannot respond. In contrast, YHWH breathes life into humans and fills his prophets with his rûaḥ; the divine/idol contrast is precisely the contrast between living rûaḥ and lifeless void.
18: kĕmôhem yihyû ʿōśêhem kōl ʾăšer bōṭēaḥ bāhem — "those who make them will become like them — and so will all who trust in them" — the spiritual law: worshippers are conformed to their object of worship. kĕmôhem yihyû (they shall become like them) — those who fashion and trust lifeless, speechless, sightless idols become spiritually mute and blind themselves (cf. Jer 10:14; Hos 9:10). The warning extends from craftsman to truster: the idolater's spiritual impoverishment mirrors the idol's ontological poverty.
19: bêt yiśrāʾēl bārĕkû ʾet-YHWH bêt ʾahărōn bārĕkû ʾet-YHWH — "praise the LORD, O house of Israel! Praise the LORD, O house of Aaron!" — the fourfold call to bless (vv19–21) mirrors the fourfold blessing structure of Ps 115:9–13 and the Aaronic benediction categories. bêt yiśrāʾēl (house of Israel) — the whole covenant people. bêt ʾahărōn (house of Aaron) — the Aaronic priesthood specifically, the descendants of Aaron who officiated in the sanctuary.
20: bêt hallēwî bārĕkû ʾet-YHWH yirʾê YHWH bārĕkû ʾet-YHWH — "praise the LORD, O house of Levi! All who fear the LORD, praise the LORD!" — bêt hallēwî (house of Levi) — the broader Levitical tribe, including temple singers, porters, and servants not of Aaronic descent. The four groups form a concentric pattern: all Israel → priests (Aaron) → Levites → God-fearers. yirʾê YHWH (those who fear the LORD) is the same phrase used in Ps 22:23; 115:11, 13 — likely designating a fourth identifiable group that could include devout Gentiles and proselytes beyond ethnic Israel.
21: bārûk YHWH miṣṣiyyôn šōkēn yĕrûšālāim hallĕlû-yāh — "praise be to the LORD from Zion — he who makes his home in Jerusalem. Praise the LORD!" — šōkēn (dwelling, making his home, participle of šākan) — the same root as the Miškan (tabernacle) and the later concept of the Shekinah; YHWH is not merely worshipped in Zion but resident there. The closing hallĕlû-yāh creates an envelope with the opening v1, framing the entire psalm as a praise bracket around the historical and theological content of vv4–20. The Davidic covenant — YHWH dwelling in Zion — anchors the cosmic creator's presence in a specific place.
